topics.title: Whole project along with layout of treatment plant and distribution network to be presented before the panel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCVT404 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING I
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Whole project along with layout of treatment plant and distribution network to be presented before the panel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Water Supply Treatment Plant Project Presentation** is a comprehensive technical submission that consolidates the engineering design of a municipal/rural water supply scheme, encompassing the **schematic layout of unit operations** in the treatment plant, the **hydraulic profile** of sequential treatment units, the **distribution network geometry** (pipelines, valves, service reservoirs, and elevated tanks), population forecasts, demand estimates, cost analysis (capital and recurring), and operational/maintenance manuals, all presented to a **Panel of Examiners** for evaluation under the KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education framework.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus:** The student is expected to integrate the design principles of Module 1 (Water Quality & Demand), Module 2 (Treatment Units), Module 3 (Pumps & Conduits), and Module 4 (Network + Project) into a **single, coherent, presentation-ready engineering project**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital for Water"

Think of a water treatment plant as a **multi-specialty hospital for raw river water**:

- **Intake (Patient Admission):** Raw water is "admitted" from the source.
- **Screening (Triage):** Large debris is removed.
- **Sedimentation (General Ward):** Heavy particles settle.
- **Filtration (ICU):** Fine impurities are removed.
- **Disinfection (Discharge):** The water is "cured" and discharged into the city.

The **distribution network** is the **ambulance service** — it carries the "treated patient" to every household with the correct pressure, ensuring no contamination en route.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) for Panel Evaluation:**
> - **Turbidity removal efficiency** $\geq$ **95%**
> - **Residual chlorine** = **0.2 to 0.5 mg/L**
> - **Pressure head in network** $\geq$ **7 m to 15 m** (as per CPHEEO)
> - **Per capita demand met** = **135 LPCD** (minimum for towns, Kerala context)

## 1.3 Scope of the Project Presentation

The presentation must cover the following **mandatory deliverables** before the panel:

| Deliverable | Description | Approx. Weightage |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Project Synopsis** | Source, location, population forecast | 5% |
| **2. Demand Analysis** | Per capita demand, peak factor, fire demand | 10% |
| **3. Treatment Plant Layout** | Plan + Hydraulic profile (L-section) | 25% |
| **4. Distribution Network** | Pipe sizing, node analysis, zoning | 25% |
| **5. Cost Estimates** | Capital, O&M, unit water cost | 15% |
| **6. Drawings & CAD Output** | Plan, L-section, schematic diagrams | 15% |
| **7. Q&A / Viva Response** | Panel interaction | 5% |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of the Project

A complete environmental engineering project is a **synthesis of three pillars**:

### Pillar 1: **Source & Conveyance**
- Selection of source (surface / subsurface).
- Intake well design.
- Pumping main (rising main) sizing using **Hagen-Williams equation**.

### Pillar 2: **Treatment Plant Layout (Process Engineering)**
- **Hydraulic Flow Pattern:** Gravity flow is preferred.
- **Unit Operation Sequencing:** Screening $\rightarrow$ Aeration $\rightarrow$ Coagulation-Flocculation $\rightarrow$ Sedimentation $\rightarrow$ Filtration $\rightarrow$ Disinfection.
- **Land Requirement:** Approximately **0.05 to 0.10 hectares per MLD** of installed capacity.
- **Hydraulic Profile:** Difference between FSL of source and FSL of clear water reservoir must overcome head losses across all units.

### Pillar 3: **Distribution Network**
- **Continuous System** (no pumps, gravity fed): Most common in Kerala.
- **Zoned Distribution:** City divided into DMA (District Metered Areas).
- **Network Topology:** Branch, Grid, or Ring system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a Layout Matters to the Panel:**
> The layout is the **visual proof of engineering competence**. It demonstrates that the student understands:
> 1. **Process Integration** (units work together, not in isolation).
> 2. **Hydraulic Logic** (water flows by gravity, no unnecessary pumping).
> 3. **Land Economics** (minimum footprint, future expansion possible).
> 4. **Operational Safety** (bypasses, washouts, overflows included).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Population Forecast (Arithmetic) | $P_n = P_0 + n \cdot \bar{x}$ | persons | $\bar{x}$ = decadal growth |
| 2 | Population Forecast (Geometric) | $P_n = P_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{100}\right)^n$ | persons | $r$ = growth rate % |
| 3 | Population Forecast (Incremental) | $P_n = P_0 + n \cdot x + \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} \cdot y$ | persons | Used for new towns |
| 4 | Per Capita Demand | $Q_{avg} = P \cdot q$ | L/day | $q$ = LPCD |
| 5 | Peak Factor (Kuichling) | $PF = 1.8 + \dfrac{2.67}{P^{0.2}}$ | – | Empirical (USA) |
| 6 | Peak Factor (CPHEEO) | $PF = 2.5$ for towns | – | Indian standard |
| 7 | Fire Demand (Kuchling) | $Q_f = 3182 \sqrt{P}$ | L/min | For $P$ in thousands |
| 8 | Fire Demand (Buston's) | $Q_f = 5663 \sqrt{P}$ | L/min | Indian code |
| 9 | Total Demand (Coincident) | $Q_{max} = 1.8 \cdot Q_{avg} + Q_f$ | L/min | Add fire to peak |
| 10 | Pipe Sizing (Hagen-Williams) | $h_f = \dfrac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot D^{4.87}}$ | m | $Q$ in m³/s |
| 11 | Pipe Sizing (Manning's) | $h_f = \dfrac{10.29 \cdot n^2 \cdot L \cdot Q^2}{D^{5.33}}$ | m | For concrete / CI |
| 12 | BOD Design | $D = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{X \cdot (1 + k \cdot t_c)}$ | kg/day | Activated sludge |
| 13 | Filter Sizing | $A = \dfrac{Q}{v_f}$ | m² | $v_f$ = 5–15 m/h |
| 14 | Sedimentation (Surface Loading) | $v_0 = \dfrac{Q}{A_s}$ | m/h | $\leq$ 1.5 m/h |
| 15 | Detention Time | $t = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | h | 2–4 h typical |
| 16 | Hazen-Williams C-value | DI/CI = 100–130 | – | New pipes |
| 17 | Cost per MLD (Empirical) | $C = a + b \cdot (Q)^{0.7}$ | ₹ Lakhs | Plant cost index |
| 18 | Unit Water Cost | $UWC = \dfrac{A + P \cdot CRF + O\&M}{Annual\ Water\ Sold}$ | ₹/kL | CRF = capital recovery |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constraint Checklist (Panel Standard):**
> - Min pressure at consumer: **7 m** (single storey) / **17 m** (multi-storey)
> - Max velocity in pipe: **2.5 m/s** (to prevent surge)
> - Min velocity in pipe: **0.6 m/s** (to prevent silting)
> - Pipe diameter (mains): $\geq$ **100 mm** (rural), $\geq$ **150 mm** (urban)

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

A treatment plant layout presented before a panel mirrors the **actual project approval** submitted to:
- **Kerala State Pollution Control Board (KSPCB)**
- **Public Health Engineering Department (PHED)**
- **Central Public Health and Environmental Engineering Organisation (CPHEEO)**
- **Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM)** for rural Kerala schemes

The distribution network design, when presented accurately, is **directly translatable** to **GIS-based SCADA systems** used by **Kerala Water Authority (KWA)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Peak Factor (Kuichling's Formula) — Justification

Kuichling's empirical relation is widely used by KTU evaluators. The **derivation logic** is based on regression of observed maximum-day demand curves across US cities.

**Step 1 — Base Relation:**
Kuichling observed that peak factor decreases as population increases, following a power law:

$$
PF_{max} = A \cdot P^{-B}
$$

where $A$ and $B$ are empirical constants.

**Step 2 — Curve Fitting:**
From the observed data (1880–1900 US cities), Kuichling obtained:

$$
A = 4.7, \quad B = 0.2
$$

**Step 3 — Adjustment for Minimum:**
For very small populations, $PF \to \infty$. To prevent this, a constant is added:

$$
PF = 4.7 \cdot P^{-0.2} + 1.0
$$

**Step 4 — Simplification (commonly used form):**
Combining the constants yields the working formula:

$$
PF = 1.8 + \frac{2.67}{P^{0.2}}
$$

where $P$ is in thousands.

**Step 5 — Verification (Example):**
For $P = 50{,}000$:

$$
P \text{ in thousands} = 50
$$

$$
PF = 1.8 + \frac{2.67}{50^{0.2}}
$$

$$
50^{0.2} = 50^{1/5} \approx 2.187
$$

$$
PF = 1.8 + \frac{2.67}{2.187} = 1.8 + 1.221 = 3.021
$$

**Conclusion:** For a town of 50,000 people, peak factor $\approx$ **3.0**, which agrees with CPHEEO recommended value of 2.5 for Indian conditions — the slight difference arises from climatic and consumption pattern variations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Pipe Diameter by Hazen-Williams (Economic Sizing)

**Step 1 — Discharge Equation:**
Hazen-Williams gives head loss as:

$$
h_f = \frac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot D^{4.87}}
$$

**Step 2 — Head Loss Available (Grade Condition):**
For gravity flow between two reservoirs with difference in level $\Delta H$ and length $L$:

$$
h_f = \Delta H \quad \text{(fully utilized)}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $D$:**

$$
D^{4.87} = \frac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot \Delta H}
$$

$$
D = \left[ \frac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot \Delta H} \right]^{1/4.87}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Practical Form:**
Substituting numerical exponent $1/4.87 \approx 0.2053$:

$$
D = 1.626 \cdot \left[ \frac{L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot \Delta H} \right]^{0.2053}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked Example:**
Given $L = 1000$ m, $Q = 0.05$ m³/s, $C = 130$ (CI pipe), $\Delta H = 5$ m:

$$
D = 1.626 \cdot \left[ \frac{1000 \cdot 0.05^{1.852}}{130^{1.852} \cdot 5} \right]^{0.2053}
$$

$$
0.05^{1.852} \approx 0.00336
$$

$$
130^{1.852} \approx 11{,}295
$$

$$
D = 1.626 \cdot \left[ \frac{1000 \cdot 0.00336}{11{,}295 \cdot 5} \right]^{0.2053} = 1.626 \cdot \left[ 5.95 \times 10^{-5} \right]^{0.2053}
$$

$$
5.95 \times 10^{-5 \, \cdot \, 0.2053} = (5.95 \times 10^{-5})^{0.2053} \approx 0.181
$$

$$
D \approx 1.626 \cdot 0.181 \approx 0.294 \text{ m} = 294 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Adopt Standard Diameter:**
The next commercially available standard is **300 mm** (nominal). Hence adopt $D = 300$ mm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Panel Tip:** Always check velocity after sizing. Here, $V = Q/A = 0.05 / (\pi/4 \cdot 0.3^2) = 0.707$ m/s, which lies within the safe range of 0.6–2.5 m/s. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Filter Bed Area and Number of Units

**Step 1 — Given:**
Plant capacity $Q = 10$ MLD, Filtration rate $v_f = 6$ m/h.

**Step 2 — Convert Units:**

$$
Q = 10 \times 10^3 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} = \frac{10{,}000}{24} \text{ m}^3/\text{h} = 416.67 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}
$$

**Step 3 — Filter Area:**

$$
A = \frac{Q}{v_f} = \frac{416.67}{6} = 69.44 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Number of Units:**
Using standard filter area of 20 m² (5 m × 4 m):

$$
N = \frac{69.44}{20} = 3.47 \approx 4 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Adjusted Area (with one standby):**

$$
N = 4 + 1 = 5 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 6 — Revised Filtration Rate with Standby:**

$$
A_{active} = 4 \times 20 = 80 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
v_f = \frac{416.67}{80} = 5.21 \text{ m/h} \quad \text{(acceptable, < 6 m/h)}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Treatment Units

| Raw Water Characteristic | Primary Treatment | Secondary Treatment | Tertiary |
|---|---|---|---|
| Low turbidity (< 50 NTU) | Plain Sedimentation | Pressure Filtration | Disinfection |
| Medium turbidity (50–500 NTU) | Coagulation + Sedimentation | Rapid Sand Filtration | Cl₂ / UV |
| High turbidity (> 500 NTU) | Pre-chlorination + Sedimentation | Dual-media Filtration | Ozonation |
| Hard water | – | Lime-Soda Softening | Stabilization |
| Iron & Manganese | Aeration | Pressure Sand Filtration | Oxidation |
| Microbial contamination | – | Slow Sand Filtration | UV / Cl₂ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Treatment Plant Layout — Schematic Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Intake Well at Source] --> A2[Rising Main - Pump Station]
    A2 --> A3[Coarse Screen - Bar Rack]
    A3 --> A4[Fine Screen - 6mm Aperture]
    A4 --> A5[Raw Water Pumping Station]
    A5 --> A6[Aeration Fountain]
    A6 --> A7[Flash Mixer - Coagulant Dosing]
    A7 --> A8[Clariflocculator - 4 Hrs Detention]
    A8 --> A9[Filter Inlet Channel]
    A9 --> A10[Rapid Sand Filter - Bed 1]
    A9 --> A11[Rapid Sand Filter - Bed 2]
    A9 --> A12[Rapid Sand Filter - Bed 3]
    A10 --> A13[Pure Water Channel]
    A11 --> A13
    A12 --> A13
    A13 --> A14[Chlorine Contact Tank - 30 min]
    A14 --> A15[Clear Water Reservoir - 4 Hours Capacity]
    A15 --> A16[Blower Room for Filter Backwash]
    A16 --> A17[Chemical House - Alum Lime Chlorine]
    A15 --> A18[Distribution Pump House]
    A18 --> A19[Elevated Service Reservoir ESR]
    A19 --> A20[Distribution Network - Zone 1]
    A19 --> A21[Distribution Network - Zone 2]
    A20 --> A22[Domestic Consumer - 7 m Head]
    A21 --> A23[Industrial Consumer - 17 m Head]
```

## 4.2 Treatment Plant — Hydraulic Profile (L-Section)

```mermaid
graph LR
    L1[Source River - Level 100.00 m] --> L2[Intake Well 95.00 m]
    L2 --> L3[Raw Water Pump House 95.50 m]
    L3 --> L4[Aeration Unit 98.00 m]
    L4 --> L5[Flash Mixer 97.80 m]
    L5 --> L6[Clariflocculator 96.50 m]
    L6 --> L7[Filter Bed Top 96.20 m]
    L7 --> L8[Filter Bed Bottom 95.20 m]
    L8 --> L9[Pure Water Sump 95.00 m]
    L9 --> L10[Chlorine Contact Tank 94.80 m]
    L10 --> L11[Clear Water Reservoir 93.50 m]
    L11 --> L12[Distribution Pump 94.00 m]
    L12 --> L13[ESR Overflow 110.00 m]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Hydraulic Profile:**
> - Water always moves from a **higher level** to a **lower level** under gravity.
> - Total head loss across all units $\approx$ **6.5 m** in this profile.
> - FSL of source must be $\geq$ ESR top level + 6.5 m (to drive flow by gravity).

## 4.3 Distribution Network — Grid Topology

```mermaid
graph TD
    Z1[ESR Zone 1 - 500 kL] --> P1[Primary Main 300 mm DI]
    Z1 --> P2[Primary Main 300 mm DI]
    P1 --> S1[Secondary Main 150 mm]
    P1 --> S2[Secondary Main 150 mm]
    P2 --> S3[Secondary Main 150 mm]
    P2 --> S4[Secondary Main 150 mm]
    S1 --> T1[Tertiary Main 100 mm]
    S1 --> T2[Tertiary Main 100 mm]
    S2 --> T3[Tertiary Main 100 mm]
    S3 --> T4[Tertiary Main 100 mm]
    S4 --> T5[Tertiary Main 100 mm]
    S4 --> T6[Tertiary Main 100 mm]
    T1 --> H1[House Connection 15 mm]
    T2 --> H2[House Connection 15 mm]
    T3 --> H3[House Connection 15 mm]
    T4 --> H4[House Connection 15 mm]
    T5 --> H5[House Connection 15 mm]
    T6 --> H6[House Connection 15 mm]
```

## 4.4 Project Presentation Workflow — Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Step 1 Problem Definition] --> P2[Step 2 Data Collection]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 Population Forecast]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 Demand Estimation]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5 Treatment Plant Design]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6 Distribution Network Design]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7 Hydraulic Profile]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8 Cost Estimation]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9 Layout Drawings]
    P9 --> P10[Step 10 Report Compilation]
    P10 --> P11[Step 11 Panel Presentation]
    P11 --> P12[Step 12 Q and A Response]
```

## 4.5 Functional Architecture Mapping — Treatment Plant Sections

```mermaid
graph TD
    SecA[Section A - Headworks] --> SecA1[Receives raw water]
    SecA1 --> SecA2[Screenings disposal yard]
    SecA --> SecB[Section B - Pretreatment]
    SecB --> SecB1[Aerator and Flash mixer]
    SecB --> SecB2[Chemical dosing units]
    SecB --> SecC[Section C - Sedimentation]
    SecC --> SecC1[Clariflocculator basins]
    SecC --> SecC2[Sludge thickener]
    SecC --> SecD[Section D - Filtration]
    SecD --> SecD1[Filter beds and backwash]
    SecD --> SecD2[Filter house]
    SecD --> SecE[Section E - Disinfection]
    SecE --> SecE1[Chlorination contact tank]
    SecE --> SecF[Section F - Storage and Pumping]
    SecF --> SecF1[Clear water reservoir]
    SecF --> SecF2[High service pump house]
    SecF --> SecG[Section G - Distribution]
    SecG --> SecG1[ESR and gravity mains]
    SecG --> SecG2[District metered areas]
    SecG1 --> SecH[Section H - Admin and Lab]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List the major components of a conventional water treatment plant and state the purpose of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A conventional water treatment plant has the following major components:

1. **Intake Works (1 mark):** To withdraw raw water from the source (river/lake) and deliver it to the treatment plant.

2. **Screening Unit (0.5 mark):** To remove floating and large suspended debris like leaves, branches, plastics using bar racks (coarse and fine).

3. **Sedimentation / Clariflocculator (0.5 mark):** To remove suspended solids by gravity settling; coagulants like alum are dosed to enhance settling.

4. **Filtration Unit (0.5 mark):** To remove residual fine suspended particles using rapid sand filters, achieving turbidity < 1 NTU.

5. **Disinfection Unit (0.5 mark):** To destroy pathogenic microorganisms using chlorine, achieving residual chlorine of 0.2–0.5 mg/L at the consumer end.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What are the key parameters to be included in a treatment plant hydraulic profile? Why is it important?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A hydraulic profile (L-section) is a longitudinal vertical cross-section showing the **water surface elevation** at every point in the treatment train.

**Key Parameters (2 marks):**
- FSL (Full Supply Level) of source
- Invert levels of conduits, channels, and tanks
- FSL of each treatment unit
- Head loss across each unit
- FSL of clear water reservoir
- FSL of ESR and HGL of distribution network

**Importance (1 mark):**
The hydraulic profile ensures **gravity flow** is maintained throughout the plant, eliminates unnecessary pumping cost, and verifies whether the available head is sufficient to drive flow through all units without choking.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a) Design the layout of a 10 MLD conventional water treatment plant for a Kerala town. List all unit operations, their detention times, and the land area required. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Plant Capacity:**
$Q = 10$ MLD $= 10{,}000$ m³/day $= 416.67$ m³/h

**Step 2 — Unit Design (7 marks):**

| Unit | Detention / Rate | Volume / Area | Dimensions | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Intake Well** | 2 min | 13.9 m³ | 3 m dia × 2 m deep | 0.5 |
| **Screen** | Velocity 0.6 m/s | Bar 25 mm clear | 2 channels × 1.5 m wide | 0.5 |
| **Flash Mixer** | 60 seconds | 6.94 m³ | 2 m × 2 m × 1.8 m | 1.0 |
| **Clariflocculator** | 4 hours | 1666.7 m³ | 2 units, 12 m dia × 3.5 m SWD | 1.5 |
| **Rapid Sand Filter** | Rate 6 m/h | 69.4 m² | 4 units (5 m × 4 m) | 1.5 |
| **Chlorine Contact Tank** | 30 minutes | 260 m³ | 10 m × 4 m × 6.5 m | 1.0 |
| **Clear Water Reservoir** | 4 hours | 1666.7 m³ | 25 m × 13.3 m × 5 m | 1.0 |

**Step 3 — Total Land Area:**

$$
\text{Working area} \approx 1.0 \text{ hectare}
$$

$$
\text{Buffer + future expansion} = 50\% \text{ extra}
$$

$$
\text{Total land required} = 1.5 \text{ hectares (minimum 2.0 ha recommended)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity check:** Standard empirical rule is 0.1 ha/MLD. Hence, for 10 MLD, land needed $\approx$ 1.0 ha (working) + 0.5 ha (future) = 1.5 ha. ✓

---

**(b) Draw the schematic flow diagram of a conventional water treatment plant and explain the function of each unit. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The schematic flow diagram (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes) consists of the following sequential units, each serving a specific function:

1. **Intake Well (1 mark):** Acts as the entry point; ensures continuous abstraction with adequate submergence for pumps.

2. **Screening (1 mark):** Removes large floating matter; coarse screen (50 mm) → fine screen (6 mm). The screenings are disposed of by burial or incineration.

3. **Aeration (1 mark):** Removes dissolved gases (CO₂, H₂S), oxidizes Fe²⁺ and Mn²⁺, and adds oxygen to improve taste.

4. **Flash Mixer (1 mark):** Rapid mixing of coagulant (alum) for 30–60 seconds; destabilizes colloidal particles.

5. **Clariflocculator (1 mark):** Two-zone tank — flocculation zone (slow mixing, 20–30 min) and clarification zone (2–4 h settling). Produces sludge at bottom, clarified water on top.

6. **Rapid Sand Filter (1 mark):** Removes remaining suspended particles; filter media — sand (0.45–0.7 mm), gravel support, underdrain system. Backwash every 24–48 h.

7. **Chlorine Contact Tank (1 mark):** Provides 30 minutes contact time for chlorine to inactivate bacteria, viruses, and protozoan cysts.

> [!NOTE]
> The complete treated water is stored in the clear water reservoir and pumped to the Elevated Service Reservoir (ESR) for gravity distribution.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a) Design a distribution network for a residential zone of 50,000 population with per capita demand of 150 LPCD. Calculate peak demand, fire demand, and total main diameter using Hazen-Williams. Assume pipe length 2 km, available head 8 m, C = 120. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Average Demand:**

$$
Q_{avg} = P \times q = 50{,}000 \times 150 = 7{,}500{,}000 \text{ L/day} = 7500 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

**Step 2 — Peak Demand (Kuichling's Formula):**

$$
PF = 1.8 + \frac{2.67}{P^{0.2}}, \quad P = 50
$$

$$
50^{0.2} = 2.187
$$

$$
PF = 1.8 + \frac{2.67}{2.187} = 1.8 + 1.221 = 3.021
$$

$$
Q_{peak} = 3.021 \times 7500 = 22{,}657 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} = 22{,}657/86400 = 0.262 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Fire Demand (Kuchling's Formula):**

$$
Q_f = 3182 \sqrt{P_{thousand}} = 3182 \times \sqrt{50} = 3182 \times 7.071 = 22{,}500 \text{ L/min}
$$

$$
Q_f = 22{,}500 / 60 = 375 \text{ L/s} = 0.375 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Coincident Design Demand:**

$$
Q_{design} = 1.8 \times Q_{avg} + Q_f = 1.8 \times 0.0868 + 0.375 = 0.156 + 0.375 = 0.531 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — Pipe Diameter (Hazen-Williams):**

$$
D = \left[ \frac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot h_f} \right]^{1/4.87}
$$

With $L = 2000$ m, $Q = 0.531$ m³/s, $C = 120$, $h_f = 8$ m:

$$
Q^{1.852} = 0.531^{1.852} \approx 0.319
$$

$$
C^{1.852} = 120^{1.852} \approx 9{,}884
$$

$$
D = \left[ \frac{10.67 \times 2000 \times 0.319}{9884 \times 8} \right]^{0.2053} = \left[ \frac{6808}{79{,}072} \right]^{0.2053} = [0.0861]^{0.2053} \approx 0.612 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6 — Adopt Standard Diameter:**
Use **600 mm DI pipe** (standard commercially available).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often use the peak demand **alone** and forget to add fire demand. In a residential zone, fire demand can exceed peak domestic demand, so always compute $Q_{design} = 1.8 Q_{avg} + Q_f$ and use the larger of peak or design value.

---

**(b) Explain the components of a water treatment plant layout drawing. List the mandatory features that must be shown in the layout presented before the panel. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A water treatment plant layout drawing consists of two main views:

**1. Plan View (Top View) (3.5 marks):**
- **Boundary wall and gate** of the plant.
- **All unit tanks** drawn to scale (intake, screen, clariflocculator, filters, chlorine contact tank, CWR).
- **Pipelines** with diameters marked (raw water, filtered water, sludge, wash water).
- **Walkways, roads, and green belts** for accessibility.
- **Location of high-tension line** and **electrical substation**.
- **Admin building, lab, staff quarters, parking**.
- **Bypass channels** for emergencies.
- **Sludge drying beds** and **chemical storage godown**.

**2. L-Section (Hydraulic Profile) (3.5 marks):**
- **Reduced Levels (RL)** of every tank and channel.
- **Flow direction** indicated by arrows.
- **Head loss** at each unit.
- **FSL of source, ESR, and CWR** for verification of gravity flow.
- **Pipe slopes** in the conveyance system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Features for Panel Approval:**
> 1. **North direction mark** and **scale bar.**
> 2. **Title block** with project name, drawing number, date, and student details.
> 3. **Colour coding** of pipelines (e.g., blue for raw water, green for treated water, brown for sludge).
> 4. **Section line A-A'** for hydraulic profile.
> 5. **Future expansion zone** marked with dotted line.
> 6. **Invert levels** of every conduit clearly stated.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons for Losing Marks in Project Presentation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Fire Demand:** Domestic peak alone is insufficient. Always add fire demand **coincidentally** (Kuchling's formula).
>
> 2. **Wrong Units in Hazen-Williams:** Mixing m³/s with L/s, or m with cm. **Always convert** before substitution.
>
> 3. **No Hydraulic Profile:** Many students submit only the plan. A project without L-section is considered **incomplete** and may lose up to **4–5 marks**.
>
> 4. **Ignoring Sludge Disposal:** Panel penalises designs where sludge handling is not addressed. Always include **sludge drying beds** or **sludge thickening unit**.
>
> 5. **No Bypass Arrangement:** Every unit must have a **bypass channel** for maintenance. Layout without bypass is marked down.
>
> 6. **Cost Estimate Omission:** Per MLD cost and unit water cost (₹/kL) are mandatory.
>
> 7. **Improper Scale and North Mark:** Drawing without scale is **not acceptable** for a panel presentation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick Revision Checklist (Must Memorize for KTU 2024 Exam):**

- **Per Capita Demand (Kerala context):** **135 LPCD** minimum, **150 LPCD** with full coverage.
- **Population Forecasting Methods:** Arithmetic, Geometric, Incremental Increase — choose based on town age.
- **Peak Factor:** Kuichling's formula gives **2.5–3.5** for typical towns; CPHEEO recommends **2.5**.
- **Fire Demand:** Kuchling's $Q_f = 3182 \sqrt{P}$ (in L/min, $P$ in thousands).
- **Pipe Velocity Limits:** 0.6 m/s (min) to 2.5 m/s (max).
- **Filter Rate:** 5–6 m/h (rapid sand gravity filter).
- **Sedimentation Detention Time:** 2–4 hours.
- **Chlorine Contact Time:** **30 minutes minimum.**
- **Clear Water Reservoir Capacity:** **4 hours** of average daily demand.
- **Hazen-Williams C-value:** 130 (CI new), 120 (CI old), 100 (cement concrete), 140 (uPVC).
- **Land Required for WTP:** **0.1 hectare per MLD** (working area only).
- **Distribution Pressure:** Min 7 m at consumer tap (single storey).
- **Maximum pipe diameter in network:** 300–600 mm (primary), 100–150 mm (secondary).
- **ESR Capacity:** 1/3 to 1/4 of total daily demand.
- **Sludge Drying Bed Area:** 0.05 m² per capita (for ~5% solids).
- **Unit Water Cost (typical India 2024):** ₹5 to ₹15 per kL.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Panel Tips:**
> 1. Arrive 10 minutes early; keep your drawings and report in a **single bound folder**.
> 2. Prepare **3–5 minute summary** of the project (Introduction → Method → Results → Conclusion).
> 3. Be ready to defend every numerical value in your report — panels will ask "**why this diameter? why this depth?**".
> 4. Carry a **scale, protractor, and calculator** for any on-the-spot correction.
> 5. Mention **CPHEEO Manual 1999** and **IS 10500 (2012)** as your design references.
